coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites as advanced anode materials for lithium-ion batteries
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Jiande
Yuan, Yuan
Su, Qiong
Pan, Anqiang
Dinesh, Selvakumaran
Peng, Cheng
Cao, Guozhong
Liang, Shuquan
coredata.dc:description: Nb2O5 has attracted increasing attention as anode materials for lithium ion batteries (LIBs) because of its high safety and good structural stability. However, its electrochemical properties are controlled by the poor electronic conductivity and low ion diffusivity. In this work, we report the preparation of Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites overcomes these issues by a facile solvothermal method with subsequent calcination. The Nb2O5 nanoparticles are homogeneously distributed in a carbon matrix derived from carbon quantum dots. As anode materials for lithium ion batteries, the as-prepared Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites exhibit high capacity, good rate capability and cyclic stability. The Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites can deliver a stable capacity of 385 mA h g−1 after 100 cycles at 0.1 A g−1. A specific capacity of 240 mA h g−1 is maintained even after 600 cycles at 1 A g−1. The composite electrode exhibits improved electrochemical performance than pure Nb2O5 electrode. With the homogeneous distribution of the Nb2O5 in a carbon matrix derived from carbon quantum dots, the aggregation of Nb2O5 during cycles can be prevented and the electronic conductivity of Nb2O5 can be enhanced.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nb2O5
Carbon quantum dots
Nanocomposites
High-rate
Lithium-ion batteries

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: X-ray diffraction patterns (a) of precursor, Nb2O5 nanoparticles without CQDs and Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites. Raman spectrum (b) of Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites. TGA curve (c) of Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites annealed from room temperature to 650 °C at a temperature ramping rate of 10 °C min−1 in air.

SEM images of the solvothermally prepared precursor of Nb2O5 (a) and Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites (b). SEM images of Nb2O5 (c) and Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites (d). TEM (e) and HRTEM (f, g) images of Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites, inset of (g) is the corresponding SAED pattern. SEM (h) image of Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites and the corresponding elemental mapping of carbon (red), niobium (blue) and oxygen (green). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms (inset: BJH pore size distribution curve) confirm the mesoporous structure of Nb2O5 (a) and Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites (b).

The first three successive CV curves (a) of Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites electrode in the potential range of 0.01–3.0 V versus Li/Li+ at a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1. Galvanostatic discharge-charge profiles (b) for the 1st, 2nd, 5th, and 60th cycle of Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites at 0.1 A g−1. Cycle performance (c) of Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites at a current density of 0.1 A g−1 in the voltage range of 0.01–3.0 V. Rate performance (d) of Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites and pure Nb2O5 at various current rates from 0.1 A g−1 to 5.0 A g−1. High-rate cycling performance (e) of Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites and pure Nb2O5 at a current density of 1.0 A g−1 (the first three cycles at 0.1 A g−1).

(a) Nyquist plots of the Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites and Nb2O5 electrodes, and the corresponding equivalent circuit (inset). (b) Fitting of the Warburg factor (σ) from the linear fitting line of Z′ and ω−1/2 at low frequency region.
The illustration of the preparation of Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites.
Electrochemical behaviors of some previously reported Nb2O5-based materials as anode for Li-ion batteries.

row.entry: Specific capacity/mA h g−1 (cycling number)(current density)

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites as advanced anode materials for lithium-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science & Engineering, Central South University, Changsha, Hunan, 410083, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Plastic Surgery, The 3rd Xiangya Hospital of Central South University, Changsha, 410083, Hunan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The 3rd Xiangya Hospital of Central South University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science & Engineering, University of Washington, Seattle, WA, 98195, USA

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nb2O5 has attracted increasing attention as anode materials for lithium ion batteries (LIBs) because of its high safety and good structural stability. However, its electrochemical properties are controlled by the poor electronic conductivity and low ion diffusivity. In this work, we report the preparation of Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites overcomes these issues by a facile solvothermal method with subsequent calcination. The Nb2O5 nanoparticles are homogeneously distributed in a carbon matrix derived from carbon quantum dots. As anode materials for lithium ion batteries, the as-prepared Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites exhibit high capacity, good rate capability and cyclic stability. The Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites can deliver a stable capacity of 385 mA h g−1 after 100 cycles at 0.1 A g−1. A specific capacity of 240 mA h g−1 is maintained even after 600 cycles at 1 A g−1. The composite electrode exhibits improved electrochemical performance than pure Nb2O5 electrode. With the homogeneous distribution of the Nb2O5 in a carbon matrix derived from carbon quantum dots, the aggregation of Nb2O5 during cycles can be prevented and the electronic conductivity of Nb2O5 can be enhanced.

ce:section.ce:para: With the issue of environmental pollution becoming more and more critical, people are getting more and more concerned about alternative energy or renewable energy [1,2]. The issue is not only about finding a suitable alternative energy system, it is also about developing an appropriate energy storage system. For this reason, various energy storage systems have been studied in the past few years [3,4]. In particular, lithium ion batteries (LIBs) as a proverbial energy storage system have become a dominant power sources for portable electronic devices in the past decades owing to their high energy density, environmental friendliness, long cyclic life and good flexibility [5–7]. However, to meet the increasing demand, electrode materials with higher energy density, higher safety and good durability need to be investigated further.

Many kinds of transition metal oxides such as Nb2O5 [8,9], MnO [10], Co3O4 [11], V2O3 [12] and VNb9O25 [13] have been widely studied to achieve high energy density for electrode materials, due to their excellent electrochemical behaviors as anode materials for LIBs. Among them, Nb2O5 has been widely studied as an electrode material for LIBs due to its great potential, rich oxidation-reduction chemistry (Nb5+/Nb4+, Nb4+/Nb3+) and a volume expansion of less than 5% [2,14,15]. Intercalation reaction of niobium oxides are very sensitive to preparation temperature and particle size, which is similar to titanium oxide [16]. Nb2O5 has many polymorphic forms in different preparation temperature [17], such as orthorhombic (T-Nb2O5), pseudo-hexagonal (TT-Nb2O5) and monoclinic phases (M-Nb2O5). Kodama et al. [18] and Dunn's et al. [19] investigated the above-mentioned forms of Nb2O5 and revealed that the T-Nb2O5 has excellent electrochemical behaviors. Nevertheless, T-Nb2O5 still exhibits poor rate behavior due to its poor electronic conductivity and low ion diffusivity as anode materials for LIBs [20].

Great effort has been devoted to making the nanocomposites by combining the conductive materials and Nb2O5 at nanoscale. The nanostructures (such as nanobelts, nanospheres, and nanosheets) can increase the surface area of electrodes and shorten the ion diffusion path [21–23]. Furthermore, the electronic conductivity of Nb2O5 electrode can be improved by the conductive materials, such as carbon cloth [24], and graphene [25]. The majority of this strategy focus on fabricating nanostructured on conductive substrates, which always require the carefully treatment of the substrates and the good control of the reaction process. It would be interesting to explore new carbonaceous materials which can easily combine with the Nb2O5 materials during the synthesis procedure.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as an easy dispersion and small particles have attracted tremendous attention for decades [26]. CQDs with a diameter of below 10 nm have been widely applied in drug delivery, photocatalysis, bioimaging and chemical sensing, because of their high surface area, chemical stability, strong fluorescence and low toxicity [26–28]. More recently, CQDs were adsorbed on the surface TiO2 nanorods to improve their rate capability [28,29]. However, the homogenous distribution of CQDs with active material are much less reported, in particular for the active material quantum dots. Making the active material quantum dots and carbon quantum dots composite in ultra-small scale would be an inspiring strategy to get desired electrochemical performances. A carbon matrix derived from CQDs can both improve the electron conductivity and suppress the aggregation of the active materials.

Herein, we report the fabrication of Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites by a facile solvothermal route with subsequent calcination process. In this work, the as-prepared Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites exhibited high specific capacity excellent rate capability and good cyclic stability as anode materials for LIBs. With the homogeneous distribution of the Nb2O5 in a carbon matrix derived from CQDs, the aggregation of Nb2O5 during cycles can be prevented, and the electronic conductivity of Nb2O5 could be enhanced.
All chemical reagents and solvents used in this experiment were of analytical-reagent grade and were used without further purification.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were hydrothermally synthesized according to a former report [30]. In a typical synthesis, 3 mmol of sucrose (Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.) was dissolved into 30 mL of deionized water by ultrasonication for 2 h to yield a transparent solution. The obtained transparent solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and kept in an electrical oven at 160 °C for 3 h. After cooling down naturally, a brown solution was obtained. Then the brown solution was ultrasonicated for 6 h to get the CQDs solution and the obtained CQDs solution was used for further synthesis process.

Here, Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites were synthesized by a simple solvothermal and solid-state reaction. The ration between H5Nb3O10: oxalic acid set at 0.5: 2.25 was selected according to the previous work [8], the specific experiment process is as follows, 0.5 mmol H5Nb3O10 (Energy Chemical) and 2.25 mmol H2C2O4·2H2O (Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.) were dissolved in 5 mL of deionized water under vigorous stirring at 80 °C for 30 min. Subsequently, 0.5 g polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, Mw = 58 000, Alfa Aesar) was added into the above solution under vigorous stirring and the stirring was continued for several minutes until a clear solution was formed. Then, 10 mL as-prepared CQDs solution and 15 mL isopropanol ((CH3)2CHOH, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.) were added and stirred vigorously for 30 min. Finally, the obtained solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and treated in an electrical oven at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling down naturally, the synthesized precipitate was collected by centrifugation and washed by deionized water and pure ethanol several times before drying it at 70 °C overnight. The obtained precursors were annealed in a pipe furnace at 550 °C under the flow of argon atmosphere with a ramping rate of 1 °C min−1 for 4 h to obtain the Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites. For comparison, bare Nb2O5 was also prepared through a similar protocol without adding CQDs solution.

For identifying crystallographic phases, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of both samples were obtained by employing a Rigaku D/max 2500 X-ray diffractometer with non-monochromated Cu-Kα (λ = 1.54178 Å) in the range from 10° to 80°(2θ) with a scanning rate of 8° min−1. A thermogravimetric analyzer (Netzsch STA449C) was employed to collect the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) curves for determining the carbon contents of the as-prepared samples. Raman spectroscopy was recorded by using Raman spectrometer (LabRAM HR800). The morphologies and microstructure of the samples were studied by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, FEI Nova NanoSEM 230, 20 kV) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100F, 200 kV). Surface area measurements were analyzed by Brunauer - Emmett - Teller (BET) analysis using an adsorption instrument (ASAP 2460, Micromeritics) employed at 77 K. Surface area measurements of Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites was analyzed after degassing it under vacuum at 80 °C for 12 h.

The electrochemical measurements were implemented by assembling CR2016 type two-electrode coin cells. To fabricate the electrode, the as-prepared active materials (70 wt%), acetylene black (20 wt%) and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) (10 wt%) were dispersed in N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) solution to make a homogeneous slurry. The well-mixed slurry was spread on a copper foil by using an auto coating machine (MSK-AFA-Ⅲ, KJ Group) and dried at 100 °C for 12 h in a vacuum oven. The disk anodes were obtained by punching and the mass loading of the active material was around 1.2 mg cm−2 prior to cells assembly. The capacity of the electrode was calculated based on the total mass of the composite. Pure lithium metal foil was used as the counter and reference electrode, Celgard 2400 microporous polypropylene membrance was preferred as the separator, a solution of 1.0 M LiPF6 dissolving in a 1:1:1 (volume ratio) solvent mixture of ethylene carbonate (EC), dimethyl carbonate (DMC), and ethyl methyl carbonate (EMC) was used as the electrolyte, and all the coin cells were assembled in a glove-box filled with an ultra-high purity argon. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves were measured in a potential range of 0.01–3.0 V versus Li/Li+ at a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1 using an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E, China). The galvanostatic charge/discharge cycling and rate performance tests were conducted on a multichannel battery testing system (Land CT 2001A, China) in the potential range of 0.01–3.0 V versus Li/Li+. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were carried out on a ZAHNER-IM6ex electrochemical workstation (ZAHNER co., Germany) in the frequency range from 100 kHz to 0.01 Hz.

A schematic model for the preparation process of Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites is illustrated in Scheme 1 . HRTEM image of individual carbon quantum dots (CQDs) (Fig. S1) exhibits well-resolved lattice fringes with interplanar spacings of 0.20 and 0.21 nm corresponding to the (101) and (100) diffraction facets of carbon (JCPDS card NO. 41-1487, P63/mmc), respectively. The as-prepared CQDs are well-dispersed and have a diameter of around 3–5 nm. The CQDs were added into the niobium oxalate solution before hydrothermal treatment. The good stability of the carbon quantum dots in the solution ensures their homogeneous distribution in the mixture solution. During the hydrothermal process, Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites precursors were synthesized. The crystallographic structures of the as-synthesized samples can be confirmed by the X-ray diffraction (XRD) results in Fig. 1 a. The XRD records of as-synthesized samples reveal that they are in the orthorhombic phase (JCPDS card NO. 27-1003, space group: Pbam (55), a = 0.6168 nm, b = 29.312 nm, c = 3.936 nm) of Nb2O5. The strong peaks of precursor located at 22.7°, 46.6° and 55.4° can be indexed as (001), (002) and (202) planes of Nb2O5 crystalline (JCPDS card NO. 27-1003), respectively. The result indicates that Nb2O5 is readily formed in the precursor, but it has lower crystallinity. The XRD patterns of Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites and pure Nb2O5 have similar diffraction peaks, however Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites have wider peaks owing to their ultra-small size of crystallites. The Raman spectrum was employed to verify the presence of the carbon matrix derived from CQDs in the Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites as shown in Fig. 1b. The existence of the carbon is confirmed with two bands located around at 1359 (disorder-induced D-bands) and 1584 cm−1 (in-plane vibrational graphite G-bands) [31]. The intensity ratio of D-band and G-band (I D/I G) can be used to evaluate the degree of graphitization, and the I D/I G is found to be around 0.88. This indicates higher degree of graphitization and suggests that Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites possess good electric conductivity [32]. Furthermore, the characteristic Raman bands at around 245, 300 and 678 cm−1 further confirm that the as-synthesized samples are in the orthorhombic phase of Nb2O5. Specifically, the Raman bands in the range of 180–350 cm−1 and 580-740 cm−1 are attributed to the Nb-O-Nb angular deformation and Nb-O-Nb bridging bond of distorted NbO6 [33,34]. The carbon content in the Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites were studied by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) in air, and the curves are presented in Fig. 1c. From TGA curves, two obvious weight losses are observed between room temperature and 650 °C. The initial weight loss (3.61%) between 37 °C and 200 °C can be attributed to the evaporation of physically absorbed water and crystalline water [35]. The fast and large weight loss between 350 °C and 500 °C can be assigned to the burning out of the carbon matrix derived from CQDs. According to the calculated result of TGA curve, the presence of carbon content in the Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites is about 25.5%.

Fig. 2 shows the SEM, TEM, high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), and energy dispersive spectrum (EDS) images of the as-synthesized samples. From the SEM image (Fig. 2a and b), it is observed that the precursor of pure Nb2O5 and Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites are both composed of uniform spherical-like structures consisting of small size nanoparticles. In comparison with pure Nb2O5 precursor, the surface of the Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites precursor has a few sticky substances, which indicate that the CQDs have been tightly attached to the precursor of Nb2O5. It will provide a close-knit conductive network. The obtained pure Nb2O5 and Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites by the heat treatment as shown in Fig. 2c and d, they don't show distinct structural changes comparing to the precursor. It can be clearly observed that Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites have ultra-small size comparing to the pure Nb2O5 (about several hundred nanometers). It suggesting that the CQDs are beneficial to refining grain size of Nb2O5.

The nanostructure of the Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites were characterized by TEM in more detail, as shown in Fig. 2e–g. Fig. 2e evidenced many number of ultra-small sized particles but the presence of Nb2O5 and carbon matrix derived from CQDs isn't clear. Whereas, TEM image at a higher magnification (Fig. 2f) clearly reveals that carbon matrix derived from CQDs (marked with red circle) exist in Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites, and the lattice fringes with interplanar spacings of 0.20 and 0.21 nm corresponding to the (101) and (100) diffraction facets of carbon (JCPDS card NO. 41-1487, P63/mmc), respectively. The electrochemical utilization and rate capability of the Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites is expected to be better owing to the presence of the carbon matrix derived from CQDs. The HRTEM lattice image of the Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites is given in Fig. 2g, which reveals a lattice spacing of 0.39 nm corresponds to the (001) planes of the orthorhombic Nb2O5. The (001) planes as lithium ion transport pathways would have low energy barriers owing to the characteristic crystalline structure with mostly empty octahedral sites between (001) planes [19], indicating the chances of superior electrochemical behaviors. Moreover, the HRTEM image presents that the diameter of Nb2O5 in the Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites is around 5 nm (marked with yellow circle). The obtained ultra-small size Nb2O5 could be attributed to the effect of CQDs. The homogeneous distribution of the CQDs would hinder the Nb2O5 particle growth. In addition, the diffraction rings of (001) planes of Nb2O5 emerge in the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (inset of Fig. 2g), which demonstrate that the Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites have polycrystalline structure. The elemental mapping by EDS of the Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites in Fig. 2h displays that C, Nb, and O components are homogeneously distributed in nanocomposites.

The Nitrogen adsorption-desorption measurement results for inspecting the mesoporous nature of both Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites and Nb2O5 are shown in Fig. 3 . According to the IUPAC classification, the obtained isotherm curve of the Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites and Nb2O5 could be indexed to type Ⅳ isotherm. The calculated Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area of the Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites is 145.58 m2 g−1, which is more than twofold of Nb2O5 without CQDs (64.64 m2 g−1). And the BET specific surface area value of the Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites indicates Nb2O5 nanoparticles embedded in the carbon matrix. The corresponding pore volume for Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites is 0.37 cm3 g−1 and Nb2O5 is only 0.11 cm3 g−1. Furthermore, the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) pore-size-distribution curve (inset of Fig. 3b) of Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites indicates that the pore size distribution is mainly ranged from 2 to 20 nm, and pore volume is 0.37 cm3 g−1. However, the pore size distribution of Nb2O5 (inset of Fig. 3a) is mainly ranged from 3 to 10 nm, and pore volume is 0.11 cm3 g−1. More reactive sites for uptake and release of Li+ during the cycles owing to the relatively high surface area and the porous framework [36].

To investigate the electrochemical behaviors of ion intercalation into or deintercalation from Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites, the coin half-cells with pure metallic lithium as the counter electrode were assembled. Fig. 4 presents the electrochemical performances of the Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites and pure Nb2O5 as anode materials for LIBs. As shown Fig. 4a, the first three successive cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of the Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites, which were carried out in the potential window range from 0.01 V to 3.0 V versus Li/Li+ at a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1 at room temperature. The potential of reductive peak at around 0.75 V is clearly observed in the initial cycle, which disappears in the subsequent cycles. The irreversible peak leads to low coulombic efficiency in the first cycle owing to the formation of irreversible solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer and other side reactions. The subsequent CV profiles are almost coincided, presenting excellent reversibility of the electrode material for lithium ion intercalation and deintercalation. The CV curves were compared with the reported literature [9], they showed broad reduction peaks and oxidation peaks. A reversible lithium ion intercalation and deintercalation reaction in Nb2O5 can be described as equation (1) [37]:
(1)
Nb
2
O
5
+ x
Li
+
+ x
e
−
↔
Li
x
Nb
2
O
5
where x is the stoichiometric number of the intercalation/deintercalation of lithium ions. During the intercalation of lithium ions, two broad peaks range from 1.8 to 1.5 V and 1.5 V–1.0 V can be attributed to the reduction reaction of Nb5+ to Nb4+ and Nb4+ to Nb3+, respectively [38]. Meanwhile two broad peaks range from 1.0 to 1.3 V and 1.3–2.0 V can be ascribed to the oxidation reaction of Nb3+ to Nb4+ and Nb4+ to Nb5+, respectively. Furthermore, a pair redox peak can be clearly observed in the low voltage range from 0 to 0.2 V, which indicates the intercalation/deintercalation of lithium ions in CQDs.

The galvanostatic discharge-charge profiles of selected cycles (1st, 2nd, 5th and 60th) of the Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites in the potential range from 0.01 to 3.0 V (versus Li/Li+) at the current density of 0.1 A g−1 are shown in Fig. 4b. An exceptional high discharge capacity of 672 mA h g−1 at the initial discharging was observed. However, the Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites as anode have a great loss of specific capacity with an initial charge capacity of 391 mA h g−1, demonstrating an initial coulombic efficiency of 58.2%. The low coulombic efficiency may be resulting from the decomposition of electrolyte and the formation of SEI film on the electrode surface [39]. The subsequent profiles of the Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites after the first cycle are almost identical, and the coulombic efficiency of the 60th cycle is close to 100%, which suggest good kinetics and cycle stability of the as-prepared Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposite electrodes. The cycling performances of the Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites electrodes studied at a current density of 0.1 A g−1 is shown in Fig. 4c. The observed capacity loss for the first few cycle is due to the occurrence of irreversible side reactions in the electrode material [8]. A reversible capacity of 385 mA h g−1 can be retained after 100 cycles and the capacity retention is around 97.2% (in comparison with the third cycle), which is higher than the previously reported values for Nb2O5 anode [40,41]. This demonstrates that the Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites have an excellent cyclic performance due to the existence of the carbon matrix derived from CQDs.

The discharge rate capabilities of the as-prepared samples were further investigated to assess the rate performances. As displayed in Fig. 4d, the discharge profiles of the Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites were carried out at a potential range from 0.01 to 3.0 V at various current densities ranging from 0.1 to 5.0 A g−1. From Fig. 4d, it is evident that the capacities of the electrodes gradually decrease as the current densities increase. The specific capacities of the Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites are 395, 333, 271, 234, 196 and 134 mA h g−1 at current densities of 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0 and 5.0 A g−1, respectively. The reversible specific capacity can be recovered to 388 mA h g−1 after returning to the current density of 0.1 A g−1. It demonstrates excellent rate performances. For comparison, the rate capabilities of pure Nb2O5 nanoparticles were also tested. The specific capacities of the pure Nb2O5 nanoparticles are 239, 199, 153, 122, 93 and 63 mA h g−1 at current densities of 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0 and 5.0 A g−1, respectively. This suggests that the Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites have much better rate performances than that of the pure Nb2O5 nanoparticles and enhanced performances can be attributed to the high electrical conductivity of Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites.

In addition to the excellent rate performances, the Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites also exhibit a satisfactory long-term stability. The cyclic performances and the corresponding coulombic efficiencies of the Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites and the pure Nb2O5 nanoparticles at the current density of 1 A g−1 are shown in Fig. 4e. In order to activate the electrodes, the first three cycles were measured at a current density of 0.1 A g−1 in the voltage range of 0.01–3.0 V. It is clearly seen that the Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites exhibits good cyclic performances. A mild increase in the specific capacity is observed in the early dozens of cycles, which is attributed to the nanosized effect of the electrode materials [22]. Even after 600 cycles, it still exhibits 240 mA h g−1 with a little plunge in the capacity, which indicates that the electrode structure is highly stable. In addition, the coulombic efficiencies remain at almost 100% since the fourth cycle, indicating a highly reversible electrochemical reaction for lithium ion in intercalation and deintercalation process. However, the pure Nb2O5 nanoparticles as anode only retains a specific capacity value of 163 mA h g−1 after 200 cycles. It demonstrates that the Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites have much improved reversible cycling stabilities than that of the pure Nb2O5 nanoparticles, which can be ascribed to the introduction of the carbon matrix derived from CQDs.

Furthermore, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed for Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites as well as Nb2O5 for comparison to explore the origin behind of the enhanced electrochemical performance. As shown Fig. 5 a, Nyquist plots of Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites and Nb2O5 are both composed of one semicircle at high frequency regions and a slope line at the low frequency regions. The equivalent circuit model for understanding the impedance spectrum is shown in the inset of Fig. 5a. R s is represented the sum of electrolyte resistance and ohmic resistances of the cell components. R f and R ct stand for film resistance and charge-transfer resistance, respectively. CPE-1, CPE-2 and Z w represent the constant phase element of the SEI film, the double-layer capacitance and Warburg impedance, respectively [42,43]. The primary fitting parameters of Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites and Nb2O5 are listed in Table S1. It's seen that the Rct of Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites (146.6 Ω) is lower than that of Nb2O5 (349.2 Ω), indicating that the Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites have a better electric conductivity. As shown Fig. S2, the EIS of Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites at various voltages were tested, and the fitting parameters are listed in Table S2. During the first discharge, the R ct has lower value at voltages of 1.0, 0.5 and 0.005 V than that of fresh cell. The fitted value of R ct is 57.4 Ω at 3.0 V during the first-charge cycle, which is smaller than those fitted at various voltages during the first-discharge cycle. The fitting results of the Warburg factor (σ) as shown in Fig. 5b. The Z′ versus ω−1/2 slope of Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites is obviously lower than that of Nb2O5, indicating that the Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites reveal a higher Li+ diffusion coefficient than Nb2O5 (according to Equation S1) [36]. Therefore, the excellent electrochemical performance of Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites can be attributed to the carbon matrix derived from CQDs enhancing the electrical conductivity of Nb2O5 electrode, and the nanostructure is propitious to enhancement of the electrochemical activity for lithium storage due to the shortening diffusion length of Li+.

Table 1 summarizes some of the previously reported results on electrochemical behaviors of Nb2O5-based materials as anode for LIBs. Among them, monoclinic Nb2O5 can deliver a reversible capacity of 453 mAh g−1 at end of 2nd, but it only remained 346 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 after 50 cycles [9]. The Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites in our study can remain 385 mAh g−1with the current rate of 0.1 A g−1 after 100 cycles, which showed excellent electrochemical performances as LIBs electrodes. Specifically, Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites synthesized in our present work exhibited higher specific capacity, outstanding rate capability and cyclic stability, than the results reported in other literatures (Table 1). This specifies the potential of Nb2O5 as an anode material in LIB applications. The possible factors behind the excellent electrochemical performances of the Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites for LIBs can be explained as follows: (a) The introduction of the carbon matrix derived from CQDs provides a close-knit conductive network, which improves the electrical conductivity of Nb2O5 electrodes. (b) Nb2O5 nanoparticles embedded in the carbon matrix may prevent the aggregation of Nb2O5 nanoparticles during continuous cycling. (c) More reactive sites for uptake and release of Li+ during the cycles due to the relatively high surface area. (d) The unique nanostructure may shorten lithium ion and electron diffusion route, which is benefited to the fast charge transport.

In summary, the Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites as anode for LIBs have been successfully synthesized by a facile solvothermal route with subsequent calcination. The as-prepared Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites revealed excellent electrochemical performances for lithium ion storage than its counterpart when measured as anode materials. The Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites as anode for LIBs presents high specific capacitance (385 mA h g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 after 100 cycles) and excellent rate capability (134 mA h g−1 even at 5.0 A g−1). Moreover, it holds a capacity of 240 mA h g−1 even after 600 cycles with slight decrease in capacity, which demonstrates the higher stability of electrode structure. The enhanced performances of Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites are due to the introduction of the carbon matrix derived from CQDs and the unique nanostructure of the Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites. This work presents that the carbon matrix derived from CQDs can improve the electrochemical performances of Nb2O5 when used as an anode material. Further, the findings of our study revealed that Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposites can be used as promising electrode material for next-generation LIBs and other energy storage-conversion systems.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Program for New Century Excellent Talents in University (NCET-13-0594), Hunan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (2018JJ1036), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51874362), the Innovation Project of Central South University (2017CX001).
